request: Let users edit the reported totals and export the figures for the finance team.

problem.value: Finance team needs a way to correct reported figures and get them into a usable output — but 'edit' and 'export' are framed as simple actions, which they rarely are in a derived-figure system.
problem.evidence: "Let users edit the reported totals and export the figures for the finance team."

actors.value: 'Users' edit totals; 'finance team' consumes the export. Roles (contributor vs controller vs approver vs auditor) not distinguished.

business_objects.value: Likely: Reporting Period, Line Item/Entry, Account, Report, Export, Adjustment — but 'total' is a derived aggregate, not a stored field.
business_objects.evidence: domain note: totals are aggregated along period × account × entity

business_rules.value: Unknown whether an 'edit' produces an auditable adjustment entry or overwrites the derived value directly (override).

workflow.value: Unknown whether editing is allowed only in open/in-review periods, or whether closed periods require restatement/reopening.

integrations.value: Export target/format/consumer unspecified — could be accounting system, spreadsheet, or bank file, each with its own schema and rounding rules.

permissions.value: Unknown who may edit vs adjust vs close vs export — segregation of duties is a standing finance requirement.

config_vs_custom.value: Export formats and adjustment approval circuits are typically client-specific on this platform.
config_vs_custom.evidence: context: client-specific export formats, adjustment approval circuits

edge_cases.value: Closed-period edits, concurrent edits, retroactive corrections, rounding vs aggregation mismatches not addressed.

reporting.value: Financial reporting inherently requires an audit trail of who changed which figure, when, and why — a domain-standard compliance need, not optional.
reporting.evidence: context: "auditability of who changed which figure and why", "an audit trail on every figure that moved"

risks.value: Risk that an override decouples the report from its source entries, or that export mismatches break downstream reconciliation.

questions.q: When a user 'edits' a reported total, should this create a traceable adjustment entry (a new line that makes the sum work, kept separate from raw actuals) or should it overwrite the derived value directly (an override)?
questions.why: uncertainty × impact: this is the single fork that decides the data model and audit story — an override without a visible marker silently breaks reconciliation with source data, which the domain explicitly flags as a recurring trap.

questions.q: Can totals be edited in a closed period, or only in open/in-review periods — and if a closed figure needs correcting, is that a restatement/next-period adjustment rather than an edit?
questions.why: uncertainty × impact: closed periods are meant to be frozen; allowing free edits there invalidates prior exports and filings, so the lifecycle boundary changes what 'edit' is even allowed to mean.

questions.q: Who exactly may edit a total, who may approve that edit, and who may close the period — are these meant to be different people (segregation of duties)?
questions.why: uncertainty × impact: finance domains typically require the adjuster and the closer to be different roles; skipping this is routinely caught late at acceptance testing and forces a permissions rework.

questions.q: What is the export's destination and contract — which system/format (accounting package, bank file, spreadsheet), what field set, rounding convention, and does a past export need to stay retrievable exactly as sent?
questions.why: uncertainty × impact: export is described as a contract with a downstream consumer, not a file dump — the wrong assumption here causes rejected imports or figures that don't tie out to the penny.

questions.q: Does every edit and export need to be logged with who/when/previous-vs-new-value (audit trail), and is that trail itself something auditors will need to inspect?
questions.why: uncertainty × impact: an audit trail is a hard requirement in this domain and expensive to retrofit — worth confirming scope now rather than after build.

summary.objective: Allow authorized users to correct reported financial totals and give the finance team a reliable export of the resulting figures.
summary.scope: Likely involves: an adjustment (or override) mechanism on derived totals, period-lifecycle-aware edit rules (open vs closed), role-based edit/approve/close/export rights, an audit trail of changes, and a formatted, reproducible export to a named downstream consumer.
assumptions: Editing a total should produce an auditable adjustment entry rather than a silent override, per domain norms.
Edits are likely restricted once a period is closed, requiring restatement instead of direct mutation.
The export has a specific downstream consumer (accounting system, spreadsheet, or filing) with its own format and rounding rules.
Different roles should exist for editing, approving, and closing to preserve segregation of duties.
An audit trail of who changed which figure and why is required, not optional.
summary.blind_spot: The request treats 'edit the total' as a simple field update, but a reported total is a derived aggregate — the real decision is adjustment vs override, and getting this wrong quietly decouples the report from its own source data, undermining the very reconciliation finance relies on.

problem.value: Likely need: correct/adjust figures before finalizing a report, or let finance override a wrong aggregation before sending it out.
problem.evidence: 'edit the reported totals' implies current totals are sometimes wrong or need manual correction.

current_process.evidence: Not mentioned — no indication of how totals are corrected today (spreadsheet patch, email to controller, etc.).

actors.value: Some 'user' role edits totals; a distinct 'finance team' receives the export. Roles for who edits vs who approves vs who exports are undifferentiated.
actors.evidence: 'let users edit... export... for the finance team'.

business_objects.value: Reported Total (derived from Line Items/Entries along period×account×entity) and an Export artifact for finance.

business_rules.evidence: No indication whether an edit is a traceable adjustment entry or a raw override of the derived value — the domain's #1 trap.

workflow.evidence: No mention of period state (open/in-review/closed) — editing an open period and editing a closed one are different builds (restatement vs direct edit).

integrations.value: Export target = 'the finance team' — unclear if this is a file download, an email attachment, or a feed into another system, and in what format.
integrations.evidence: 'export the figures for the finance team' names a recipient but no format/channel/consumer contract.

permissions.evidence: No mention of who may edit vs. approve vs. export vs. merely view — segregation of duties is a named domain risk (editor should not also approve close).

config_vs_custom.evidence: Platform is multi-client/configurable; chart of accounts, export field mapping and adjustment approval circuits are typically client-specific — not addressed.

constraints.evidence: No deadline, regulatory, or dependency constraint stated, though financial reporting implies auditability obligations by default.

edge_cases.evidence: Editing totals in a closed period, concurrent edits, and re-export after a change are unaddressed — each has a defined-but-unstated correct behavior in this domain.

reporting.evidence: Financial figures require an audit trail (who changed what, why, previous vs new value) as a hard requirement in this domain — not stated but implicitly mandatory.

risks.value: Chief risk: an unmarked override silently decouples the report from its underlying entries, breaking reconciliation and audit at review time.
risks.evidence: Derived from domain trap on adjustment vs. override.

questions.q: When a user 'edits' a total, should the system create a traceable adjustment entry (new line, attributed, reversible) that keeps the total reconciled with its source data, or should it overwrite the derived value directly?
questions.why: uncertainty × impact: this is the single decision that determines the data model, the audit story, and whether the report can ever be reconciled back to source — the domain's classic trap, currently unaddressed.

questions.q: Can totals be edited only while the reporting period is open/in-review, or must edits also be possible after the period is closed (via restatement), and if so who can reopen a closed period, if anyone?
questions.why: uncertainty × impact: closed-period edit semantics change entirely (direct edit vs. next-period adjustment vs. controlled reopen) and this isn't addressed at all.

questions.q: Who is allowed to edit a total, who must approve that edit, and who receives/triggers the export — are edit, approval, and export necessarily separate roles (segregation of duties)?
questions.why: uncertainty × impact: financial domains typically require the editor of a figure not be the approver of its close; undefined roles risk a rebuild at acceptance.

questions.q: What exactly does 'export for the finance team' mean — a downloadable file (which format/columns/rounding convention), an automated feed into another system, or a retained point-in-time snapshot that must remain reproducible even after later figure changes?
questions.why: uncertainty × impact: export is described in the domain as a contract with a downstream consumer, not a file dump — the wrong assumption here breaks reconciliation and consumer validation.

questions.q: Does every edit to a total need to be logged with who changed it, when, previous vs. new value, and reason — i.e. is an audit trail a compliance requirement here?
questions.why: uncertainty × impact: an audit trail is described as a hard, not-a-nice-to-have requirement in financial reporting; retrofitting it after build is expensive.

summary.objective: Allow authorized users to correct reported figures and give the finance team a reliable exported view of those figures.
summary.scope: Likely includes: an edit mechanism for totals (adjustment entry vs. override — to be decided), respect for the reporting-period lifecycle (open vs. closed), role-based permissions for edit/approve/export, an export mechanism with a defined format/channel for finance, and an audit trail of changes to figures.
assumptions: Editing a total should be implemented as a traceable adjustment rather than a silent overwrite, to preserve reconciliation with source data (to confirm).
Totals can only be freely edited while their reporting period is open or in-review; closed periods require a restatement/adjustment path rather than direct edit (to confirm).
The 'finance team' is an internal recipient of a formatted export (file or feed), not simply a read-only screen viewer (to confirm).
Some segregation of duties exists between the person editing a total and the person approving/closing the period (to confirm).
summary.blind_spot: The request treats 'edit the total' as a simple field change, but a reported total is a derived aggregation, not a stored value — editing it without defining adjustment-vs-override semantics, period-lock rules, and an audit trail will produce a report that silently stops tying back to its own source data, which is discovered only at audit or reconciliation time.

problem.value: Finance needs a controlled way to correct reported figures that don't match operational reality, and to hand off reliable numbers to the finance team/accounting system.
problem.evidence: "Let users edit the reported totals and export the figures for the finance team" — no stated pain point, but the domain implies correction + handoff need.

current_process.evidence: Not mentioned — unknown whether corrections today happen via spreadsheet, support ticket, or not at all.

actors.value: "Users" likely means controller/analyst (edits) and finance manager (approves/exports); auditor may need read-only visibility.
actors.evidence: "Let users edit ... and export ... for the finance team" — role names not specified, only implied by domain.

business_objects.value: Reported totals = derived Line Items aggregated by Period x Account x Cost Center; export produces a Report/Export snapshot.
business_objects.evidence: "reported totals" and "figures" map to the domain's Line Item / Report / Export objects.

business_rules.value: Unresolved: is editing a total an adjustment entry (traceable, additive) or an override (replaces the derived value)? Rule may also vary by account/period state.
business_rules.evidence: "Let users edit the reported totals" — the request treats this as a simple write, which the domain flags as never simple.

workflow.value: Unknown whether edits are allowed only in open/in-review periods, or also require a restatement path once a period is closed.
workflow.evidence: No mention of period state; domain treats closed-period editing as a fundamentally different flow (restatement vs adjustment).

integrations.value: Export target assumed to be an accounting system or spreadsheet for "the finance team"; format/field mapping/rounding convention unknown.
integrations.evidence: "export the figures for the finance team" — destination named loosely, no format/contract specified.

permissions.value: Unclear who may edit vs. approve vs. close vs. merely view — segregation of duties is a named domain constraint.
permissions.evidence: "users" is undifferentiated; domain requires distinct edit/adjust/close/see rights, often scoped by entity.

config_vs_custom.value: Chart of accounts, cut-off/FX conventions and export field mapping are typically client-specific.
config_vs_custom.evidence: Not addressed; platform context flags these as configurable per client.

constraints.value: Likely regulatory pressure: audit trail on any figure change, segregation of duties between adjuster and closer.
constraints.evidence: "finance team" + "reported totals" in this domain implies auditability and compliance obligations even though not explicitly requested.

edge_cases.value: Editing a figure already exported/published; rounding vs aggregation mismatch; multi-currency FX date; retroactive correction across periods.
edge_cases.evidence: Not addressed, but flagged repeatedly as recurring traps in this domain.

reporting.value: An audit trail (who edited what, when, adjustment vs override, previous vs new value) is required, not optional, given finance/compliance context.
reporting.evidence: Domain note: "an audit trail on every figure that moved" is a hard requirement for financial reporting — raises this slot above its default 'low' impact.

risks.value: Main risk: a report silently stops tying back to its source data if overrides aren't visibly marked, or a closed period is mutated without control.
risks.evidence: Inferred from domain traps around override vs adjustment and period close.

questions.q: When a user 'edits a total', should the system create a traceable adjustment entry (a new line that makes the sum add up) or an override (replacing the derived value outright)?
questions.why: uncertainty (empty) x high impact: this decides the entire data model and audit story - an override that silently disagrees with its own source data is a very different (and riskier) build than an adjustment.

questions.q: Can totals be edited once their reporting period is closed, or does a correction after close have to go through a restatement / next-period adjustment instead?
questions.why: uncertainty (empty) x high impact: closed-period edits are normally forbidden or re-routed - this changes the lifecycle and what 're-opening a period' even means.

questions.q: Who exactly may edit a figure, who may approve/close a period, and who is read-only (e.g. auditor)? Are these different people by design?
questions.why: uncertainty (empty) x high impact: segregation of duties is a named compliance constraint in this domain - conflating edit/approve/close rights is the most common gap found at acceptance.

questions.q: What is the export actually feeding - a specific accounting system, a bank/tax filing, or a spreadsheet for humans - and does it need a fixed format/field mapping the receiver validates?
questions.why: uncertainty (inferred, low completeness) x high impact: export is a contract, not a file dump; the target dictates format, rounding, and whether it must be versioned/reproducible after the fact.

questions.q: Does every figure change need a full audit trail (who, when, old vs new value, reason) to satisfy an internal or external audit?
questions.why: uncertainty (inferred) x high impact: this domain treats the audit trail as a hard requirement - confirming it now avoids an expensive retrofit later.

summary.objective: Allow authorized finance users to correct reported totals in a controlled, traceable way, and produce a reliable export of the figures for the finance team.
summary.scope: Likely includes: an adjustment-entry mechanism (not a direct field edit) tied to specific line items/accounts/periods, an audit trail of who changed what and why, permission separation between editor/approver/closer, and an export step with an agreed format/target system, versioned and retained for reproducibility.
assumptions: "Edit the total" means recording a traceable adjustment entry rather than overwriting the derived value directly.
"Users" refers to controller/analyst (editor) and finance manager (approver), with an auditor role having read-only access.
Editing is only permitted in open/in-review periods; closed periods require a restatement or next-period adjustment.
The export targets an accounting system or a finance-team spreadsheet, requiring an agreed format and being versioned/retained once sent.
An audit trail on every figure change is required for compliance, even though not explicitly requested.
summary.blind_spot: The request treats 'edit the total' as a simple write, but in this domain a reported total is a derived value: editing it is either an adjustment (traceable, additive) or an override (replaces and decouples the figure from its own source data) - two fundamentally different builds. Left unresolved, the default risk is an override with no visible marker, producing a report that silently stops tying back to the underlying data - exactly the failure mode auditors catch.
